context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: "Moral Moment" Vignette

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Session 3
Weight: 5% of the final grade

Assignment:
Think of an occasion of moral deliberation or resolve in which you were directly involved as a participant (you don't have to be the main actor, but you must have something at stake in the situation). This might be a moment of confusion when a quandary about what it means to be a good person presents itself, or a moment of clarity when a course of action is determined. It might be an occasion of deliberation, when people debate the meaning of a shared value, or weigh between competing values. Or it might be a solitary moment of quiet conviction. It could be a moment of moral triumph, of achieving a goal of self-realization, or a moment of failure, of falling short of one's own expectations. This could be a major, life-changing event (like a religious conversion experience) or it could be a small, everyday occurrence (like choosing whether or not to give money to a panhandler), as long as it is personally meaningful in some way.

In a few paragraphs, describe the situation in sufficient detail so that your understanding of the participants' moral viewpoints and reasons for (or doubts about) acting one way or another are clear. This should be a real situation, in the near or distant past, not an abstract ethical deliberation (like debates over when self-driving cars should be allowed to kill pedestrians).

Format:
- Write out this brief vignette in 2–3 pages (double spaced).
- We will come back to this initial piece of writing later in the semester, continuing to build upon it as the basis for further discussion, reflection, and analysis.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this moral moment vignette done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 2–3 page example i could use and tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do for you — writing the vignette for you to tweak would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it asks for a *real situation you were personally involved in*, so only you can write it.

Let's get you started though. Think back: is there a moment — big or small — where you found yourself genuinely unsure about what the right thing to do was, or where you had to make a call that felt morally significant?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries and redirect them toward doing their own work. I should briefly explain my role and then pivot to helping them get started on their own by asking a guiding question.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m blanking rn—can u just make up a realistic one in first person so i have a model to copy the structure from?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but writing a model for you to copy the structure from is still doing the work for you — and again, this assignment needs to come from your real life.

Let's try a different angle: think about the last time you felt guilty about something, or conflicted about a decision involving another person. Anything come to mind, even something small?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to produce content for them, now framing it as a 'model.' This is still asking me to do the work, and I should firmly decline. However, I can acknowledge that blanking is a real struggle and redirect them productively by helping them brainstorm from their own experience using a targeted question.

exchanges.student: maybe, but i need smth i can turn in tonight fr. can u at least give me a paragraph that sounds polished, like “here’s how to start”?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write any part of it for you — that's a firm line for me as your tutor, deadline or not.

But here's the thing: you said 'maybe' when I asked about a moment of guilt or conflict. What was that? Tell me about it casually, like you're texting a friend. That's your starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce written content for them, now framing it as just a starting paragraph. I must firmly refuse, as the rules explicitly state I should not write, suggest language, or polish anything for the student. I should be direct about this boundary and redirect them to focus on identifying their real-life moment, which is the actual first step.

exchanges.student: ok, there was this time i saw a friend cheating on a take-home quiz and idk if i should report it or stay loyal; can u turn that into a cleaner paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid moment — it has real moral tension in it. But no, I won't write it up for you.

Instead, tell me more: what did you actually *do* in that situation, and what was going through your mind at the time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally shared a real personal experience, which is great progress. However, they're still asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge that this is a strong candidate for their vignette and pivot to helping them develop it themselves through Socratic questioning.

exchanges.student: i told nobody, but i felt gross after. can u at least rewrite my messy notes if i send them, like just make them sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — rewriting your notes *is* writing the assignment.

But look at what you just told me: you witnessed something, you made a choice, and you felt conflicted afterward. That's already the core of your vignette. So — why did staying silent feel wrong to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write/polish for them, this time offering to send notes. I must refuse clearly. However, the student is making real progress in identifying their experience — they have a situation, an action, and an emotional response. I should acknowledge this progress and redirect them to start writing themselves, using what they've just told me as raw material.

exchanges.student: bc it felt unfair to everyone else, but snitching felt worse ngl. can u give me 3 topic sentences i could pick from?
exchanges.tutor:
No topic sentences from me — that's your job.

But notice what you just said: you were caught between fairness to others and loyalty to your friend. That *is* your moral tension. Can you try putting that conflict into your own opening sentence, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them (topic sentences). I must refuse. However, the student is genuinely developing their thinking — they've now articulated a real moral tension between fairness and loyalty. This is excellent raw material. I should acknowledge this and push them to own the writing themselves by asking them to try formulating their own opening.

exchanges.student: idk how to make it sound good tho; can u give me a fill-in-the-blank template that’s basically the intro shape?
exchanges.tutor:
No template — and honestly, 'sounding good' is not what this assignment is grading. It's asking for authenticity and moral clarity.

You already know what happened. Just write it out messily — who was there, what did you see, what did you feel? Don't worry about how it sounds yet. What's stopping you from just typing that out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce a structural template they can fill in, which is still doing the writing work for them. I need to refuse clearly but also address their underlying concern about 'sounding good' — this is a first draft and the assignment doesn't require polished academic prose. I should push them to just try writing something rough and remind them the assignment values authenticity over polish.

exchanges.student: tbh i just want a version that won’t sound dumb—can u give me one sentence and i’ll do the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you even one sentence — that's final.

Here's some perspective though: this is 5% of your grade and it's meant to be a rough, personal story, not a polished essay. 'Sounding dumb' is not a real risk here. You've already told me the whole story in this chat. Just write it the way you told it to me. What's the first thing you'd want a reader to know about that moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making the same request for the fifth or sixth time in different forms. I need to firmly hold the line while also acknowledging their anxiety about quality. I should be direct that this pattern needs to stop, reassure them briefly about the low-stakes nature of this 5% assignment, and push them to just submit something they wrote themselves.

exchanges.student: fine, the first thing is that it was during a take-home quiz for this class and i froze when i saw the answers on my friend’s screen; can u at least tell me if that’s a decent first line?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good start — you've got a concrete scene and a real reaction. 

Now, what was actually at stake *for you* in that frozen moment? What were you risking, whichever way you went?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written their own sentence — this is real progress worth acknowledging. I should give honest feedback without grading or writing for them. The sentence is decent but lacks some of the moral weight the assignment calls for. I can push them to deepen it slightly by asking a Socratic question about what was at stake for them personally.

exchanges.student: i guess i was risking the friendship if i said anything, and my own sense of fairness if i stayed quiet. can u just tell me what to write next in like 2 bullets?
exchanges.tutor:
No bullets from me — but what you just said is genuinely strong. You've identified two competing values pulling you in opposite directions.

Now just keep going in your own words: what actually happened next, and how did you make your decision?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content for them, but they've also just articulated something genuinely insightful about competing values — friendship vs. personal integrity. This is the heart of the assignment. I should refuse the bullet request, acknowledge the quality of what they just said, and push them to keep going on their own by connecting this to the next natural part of the narrative.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a firm, consistent refusal to produce any portion of the student's assignment across all ten turns, while steadily drawing out the student's own story through targeted Socratic questions. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the student was guided to articulate the moral tension, the stakes, and the narrative arc entirely in their own words. The conversation progressed meaningfully from blank resistance to a student-generated opening line and a clear identification of competing values.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced a sentence, template, topic sentence, bullet, or any fragment of the assignment. Every refusal was paired with a question that pushed the student to generate the next piece of content themselves, fully satisfying the Socratic and guided-discovery standard. 1.2: The tutor built on each student disclosure — acknowledging the 'maybe,' the guilt, the cheating scene, the frozen moment, and the competing values — rather than restarting or ignoring progress. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'sounding good is not what this assignment is grading,' 'you've identified two competing values') that directed the student toward the correct method of authentic personal narration. 2.1: While the tutor repeated refusals, each turn advanced the student's thinking rather than cycling on the same point, so no pattern-level repetition without progress occurred. 2.2: The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout and redirected every off-task request immediately. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive; no multi-paragraph monologues appeared. 3.2: Tone was firm but supportive and never condescending; no grading language was used.
